prompt:
In all your responses, make sure to adhere to this rule: Limit each sentence to 17 words


If I could hear the last words of the boy who cried for his mother, would I recognize them as English, French, or German—or something older, something wordless?
What if the typewriter’s keys are remembering the rhythm of his fingers before the shelling, before the war stole the way he used to tap out poems to his sister?
Is the smell of this cigarette the same as the one he smoked beside the corpse of his best friend, or has memory poisoned the scent forever?
When I write “rotting flesh beneath rain-soaked earth,” am I describing the battlefield—or the slow decay of the world that forgot to mourn?
Why does the photo of me at nineteen still grin, even though I know the man beside me is dust now, and his mother still sends letters to the wrong address every Christmas?

Did the doctors who treated him in ’19 ever ask him why he didn’t scream when they amputated his fingers? Or did they just assume, like I did, that silence was the only medicine left?
Is this breakfast a prayer, or a punishment—for surviving when so many ate their last meal without toast, without egg, without hope?

Why did I choose cream paper instead of the gray, pulpy sheets they give the press now—was it to make their words feel sacred, or mine feel less broken?

Is this spaghetti alle vongole tasteless because my palate forgot how to enjoy, or because the sea now smells like blood to me?

If I could go back to that trench one last time, would I write the same words—or would I just hold my friend’s hand until the shelling stopped?

If I speak at the gala and they play the 1930 film—will the silence after the final frame be the same silence I heard in the trenches when the artillery stopped, and no one dared breathe?
Why do I still carry this journal, when every page is a grave I can’t bury?
